clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman, G3P3, delivers vaginally at 39 weeks. Her labour was augmented with oxytocin for 10 hours. Immediately after delivery of the placenta, the midwife notes ongoing heavy vaginal bleeding. Estimated blood loss is 1,100 mL and rising. The uterus is soft and non-contracted on palpation. BP 90/60 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, RR 22/min, SpO2 95%. Two large-bore IV cannulae are in situ and oxygen is being administered. What is the MOST appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?
options: Ergometrine 0.5 mg IM
Misoprostol 800 mcg sublingual
Oxytocin 10 units IV bolus slowly, then start oxytocin infusion
Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) 0.25 mg IM
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV
explanation: Uterine atony is the leading cause of PPH and this woman has clear risk factors (grand multiparity, prolonged oxytocin augmentation). First-line pharmacological management is oxytocin 10 units IV (given slowly to avoid hypotension) followed immediately by an oxytocin infusion to sustain uterine tone. Bimanual uterine massage and bladder catheterisation should accompany this. Carboprost is a second-line uterotonic reserved for oxytocin failure. Tranexamic acid is an important adjunct but is not the first uterotonic. Ergometrine is contraindicated with hypertension and is second-line.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Ergometrine is a second-line uterotonic; it is contraindicated in hypertension and can cause severe vasoconstriction — oxytocin is the first-line agent.
Misoprostol is an alternative when oxytocin is unavailable, not the first choice when IV access is secured; it has more side-effects and is less effective.
Correct — oxytocin 10 units IV slowly (then infusion) is the first-line uterotonic in atonic PPH with IV access established; it is the WHO-recommended first agent.
Carboprost (PGF2α) is a potent second-line uterotonic used when first-line agents fail; jumping to it before oxytocin is not protocol-compliant.
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is an important adjunct that should be given within 3 hours of PPH, but it is not a uterotonic and does not address the atony; it comes after uterotonics.
clinicalPearl: In atonic PPH with IV access, oxytocin 10 units IV is the first-line uterotonic; give tranexamic acid 1 g IV as an early adjunct within 3 hours.
keyClue: Soft, non-contracted uterus after vaginal delivery with >1,000 mL blood loss = uterine atony requiring immediate IV oxytocin.
commonExamTrap: Choosing carboprost or ergometrine as the first pharmacological step — both are second-line; oxytocin always comes first if available.
missedConcept: The 4 Ts of PPH (Tone, Tissue, Trauma, Thrombin) — atony (Tone) is responsible for 80% of cases and is treated first with oxytocin.
redFlagReason: Massive obstetric haemorrhage with haemodynamic compromise (hypotension, tachycardia) — failure to act immediately risks maternal death from exsanguination.

clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman, G1P1, presents 12 days after an uncomplicated vaginal delivery with heavy per-vaginal bleeding, offensive lochia, and lower abdominal pain. Temperature 38.4°C, HR 102 bpm, BP 108/70 mmHg. Uterus is tender and sub-involuted on bimanual examination. Pelvic ultrasound shows a heterogeneous echogenic mass within the uterine cavity (3.2 × 2.8 cm). HCG is mildly elevated. WBC 14.2 × 10⁹/L, CRP 78 mg/L. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Endometritis without retained products
Retained products of conception with secondary infection
Gestational trophoblastic disease (choriocarcinoma)
Subinvolution of the placental site
Uterine arteriovenous malformation
explanation: The combination of secondary PPH (>24 hours post-delivery), offensive lochia, pyrexia, sub-involution of the uterus, elevated inflammatory markers, and an echogenic uterine cavity mass on ultrasound occurring 12 days postpartum is the classic presentation of retained products of conception (RPOC) complicated by secondary endometritis. Mild HCG elevation can occur with RPOC. Gestational trophoblastic disease is less likely without significantly elevated HCG and occurs later.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Pure endometritis without RPOC would not typically show an echogenic uterine mass on ultrasound; the scan finding strongly indicates retained tissue.
Correct — offensive lochia, fever, sub-involution, raised inflammatory markers, and an echogenic uterine mass 12 days post-delivery = RPOC with secondary infection.
Choriocarcinoma is rare, usually presents later with markedly elevated HCG (>100,000 IU/L), and would not typically show as a heterogeneous mass 12 days postpartum.
Subinvolution of the placental site is a diagnosis of exclusion without retained tissue on scan; it does not cause the degree of infection and inflammatory response seen here.
AVM is rare, presents with intermittent heavy bleeding without infection, and would show high-flow vascularity on Doppler — not an echogenic avascular mass.
clinicalPearl: Secondary PPH + sub-involution + offensive lochia + echogenic uterine mass on USS = retained products of conception until proven otherwise.
keyClue: Echogenic uterine cavity mass on USS + sub-involution + offensive lochia + fever at 12 days postpartum.
commonExamTrap: Attributing the presentation solely to endometritis — the ultrasound echogenic mass is the key discriminating finding pointing to RPOC.
missedConcept: Secondary PPH is defined as abnormal bleeding from 24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum; RPOC and endometritis are the commonest causes.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman, G2P2, is reviewed on the postnatal ward 18 hours after emergency Caesarean section for fetal distress. She is confused and agitated. Temperature 39.8°C, HR 128 bpm, BP 82/48 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 93% on air. Urine output over the past 2 hours is 20 mL. The wound site looks unremarkable. Lochia is offensive. Serum lactate is 4.2 mmol/L. Which SINGLE intervention is most immediately life-saving?
options: Send blood cultures and start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately
Arrange urgent pelvic ultrasound to exclude retained products
Apply ice packs and give paracetamol for temperature control
Give oral co-amoxiclav and monitor closely for 6 hours
Insert urinary catheter and measure urine output hourly
explanation: This patient meets criteria for septic shock (infection source + hypotension + lactate >4 mmol/L + organ dysfunction including confusion and oliguria). The single most life-saving intervention in septic shock is immediate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics — every hour of delay increases mortality by approximately 7%. Blood cultures must be taken before antibiotics but should not delay antibiotic administration beyond 1 hour. This patient requires the full Sepsis Six bundle, but antibiotics are the pivotal intervention.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — blood cultures immediately followed by broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam + metronidazole) is the single most life-saving step in puerperal septic shock; every hour of delay worsens mortality.
Ultrasound may be needed to identify source but is not immediately life-saving and must not delay resuscitation and antibiotics in a haemodynamically unstable patient.
Antipyretics and cooling address only the temperature; they do not treat infection or reverse septic shock — this approach is dangerously inadequate.
Oral antibiotics are entirely inappropriate in septic shock with haemodynamic compromise and altered consciousness; IV broad-spectrum antibiotics are mandatory.
Hourly urine monitoring is part of the Sepsis Six bundle but inserting a catheter alone does not treat the underlying sepsis; it is monitoring, not treatment.
clinicalPearl: Puerperal sepsis with septic shock (lactate >4, hypotension, confusion, oliguria) — give IV antibiotics within 1 hour; every hour of delay increases mortality by ~7%.
keyClue: Postpartum patient: confusion + hypotension + lactate 4.2 mmol/L + offensive lochia = septic shock requiring immediate IV antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Prioritising investigations (USS, cultures) over immediate antibiotic administration — cultures are drawn first but must not delay antibiotics beyond 1 hour.
missedConcept: Septic shock diagnostic criteria: suspected infection + organ dysfunction (confusion, oliguria) + hypotension + lactate >2 mmol/L despite resuscitation.
redFlagReason: Puerperal septic shock (lactate >4, BP 82/48, altered consciousness, oliguria) — failure to administer IV antibiotics immediately risks irreversible multi-organ failure and death.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old primipara presents 5 days after normal vaginal delivery with a 2-day history of lower abdominal pain, fever, and heavy offensive-smelling lochia. Temperature 38.9°C, HR 96 bpm, BP 118/76 mmHg. Abdominal examination reveals uterine tenderness. WBC 16.8 × 10⁹/L, CRP 112 mg/L. High vaginal swab is taken. She had a prolonged labour with multiple vaginal examinations and a manual removal of placenta under regional anaesthesia. Which organism is MOST commonly implicated as the causative agent in this presentation?
options: Staphylococcus aureus
Escherichia coli
Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes)
Bacteroides fragilis
Clostridium perfringens
explanation: Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes) is the most feared and historically the most important cause of puerperal sepsis (childbed fever). It can cause rapidly progressive, life-threatening sepsis and was responsible for the epidemic puerperal fever described by Semmelweis. Although polymicrobial infection and Gram-negative organisms also occur, Group A Streptococcus remains the pathogen most strongly associated with severe puerperal endometritis and septic shock in the immediate postnatal period. NICE and RCOG guidelines specifically highlight it.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Staphylococcus aureus causes wound infections and mastitis but is not the primary cause of endometritis/puerperal sepsis; Group A Strep is more classically implicated.
E. coli causes urinary tract infections and some cases of endometritis but is not the most classically implicated organism in puerperal sepsis.
Correct — Group A Streptococcus (S. pyogenes) is the historically and clinically most important cause of puerperal sepsis; it can spread rapidly and cause fulminant illness.
Bacteroides species are anaerobic commensals that contribute to polymicrobial pelvic sepsis but are not the primary or most classic single causative agent.
Clostridium perfringens causes gas gangrene and septic abortion but is rarely implicated in postpartum endometritis in the modern obstetric setting.
clinicalPearl: Group A Streptococcus is the most dangerous cause of puerperal sepsis — it can cause rapidly fatal septic shock and must always be covered by empirical antibiotics.
keyClue: Postpartum fever + offensive lochia + uterine tenderness 5 days post-delivery = puerperal endometritis, most classically caused by Group A Streptococcus.
commonExamTrap: Choosing E. coli because of its association with UTI — in puerperal endometritis, Group A Streptococcus is the classic and most clinically significant answer.
missedConcept: Group A Streptococcus is the leading cause of life-threatening puerperal sepsis; Semmelweis hand-washing protocols were introduced to prevent its spread.

clinicalStem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of irregular periods (cycles 45–90 days), acne, and gradual weight gain. BMI 29 kg/m². Examination reveals facial acne and mild hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score 10). Serum LH:FSH ratio is 2.8:1. Testosterone 3.2 nmol/L (upper limit of normal 2.5). Pelvic ultrasound shows bilateral enlarged ovaries with 14 follicles per ovary (2–9 mm), arranged peripherally. Thyroid function and prolactin are normal. 17-OHP is normal. Which diagnostic framework BEST supports a diagnosis of polycystic ovarian syndrome in this patient?
options: Stein-Leventhal criteria (bilateral polycystic ovaries + amenorrhoea + hirsutism)
NIH 1990 criteria (hyperandrogenism + oligo-ovulation, both required)
Rotterdam 2003 criteria (2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on USS)
AES 2006 criteria (hyperandrogenism alone required)
WHO classification of ovulatory disorders (WHO Group II)
explanation: The Rotterdam 2003 criteria require 2 out of 3 features: (1) oligo- or anovulation, (2) clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, and (3) polycystic ovarian morphology on ultrasound — after exclusion of other causes (thyroid disease, hyperprolactinaemia, CAH). This patient has all three features, and Rotterdam criteria are the currently endorsed international standard (endorsed by ESHRE/ASRM and used by most guidelines including NICE). The NIH criteria are more restrictive and would miss patients with only 2 features.
wrongAnswerExplanations: The Stein-Leventhal criteria are historical and outdated — modern practice uses Rotterdam criteria which are more inclusive and better validated.
NIH 1990 criteria require both hyperandrogenism AND oligo-ovulation; they are more restrictive than Rotterdam and would miss cases presenting with only polycystic morphology and one other feature.
Correct — Rotterdam 2003 criteria (2 of 3 features after exclusion of other causes) are the internationally endorsed standard for diagnosing PCOS and apply here.
AES 2006 criteria require hyperandrogenism as a mandatory feature plus one other; they are not the primary internationally endorsed diagnostic framework.
WHO Group II (normogonadotrophic normooestrogenic anovulation) is a classification of anovulation, not a diagnostic framework specific to PCOS.
clinicalPearl: Rotterdam 2003 criteria: diagnose PCOS with 2 of 3 features (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on USS) after excluding thyroid disease, hyperprolactinaemia, and CAH.
keyClue: Irregular cycles + clinical and biochemical hyperandrogenism + polycystic ovarian morphology on USS + normal thyroid/prolactin/17-OHP = Rotterdam-criteria PCOS.
commonExamTrap: Applying NIH criteria and requiring both hyperandrogenism AND oligo-ovulation — Rotterdam is the current standard and requires only 2 of 3 features.
missedConcept: Rotterdam criteria are more inclusive than NIH; they capture a wider PCOS phenotype spectrum, including those with polycystic morphology and hyperandrogenism but regular cycles.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman with confirmed PCOS presents requesting help with irregular periods and difficulty conceiving (trying for 18 months). She has BMI 34 kg/m², acanthosis nigricans, and fasting glucose 6.3 mmol/L. LH 12 IU/L, FSH 5.5 IU/L, testosterone 3.8 nmol/L, SHBG low. Semen analysis is normal. Fallopian tubes are patent on hysterosalpingography. After counselling about lifestyle, what is the MOST appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention for her subfertility?
options: Clomifene citrate 50 mg on days 2–6 of cycle
Letrozole 2.5 mg on days 2–6 of cycle
Metformin 500 mg twice daily alone
Gonadotrophins (FSH injections) with IUI
Combined oral contraceptive pill to regularise cycles, then reassess
explanation: NICE guideline NG245 (2023) and international PCOS guidelines now recommend letrozole (aromatase inhibitor) as first-line ovulation induction in PCOS-related anovulatory infertility, having superseded clomifene. Letrozole achieves higher live birth rates and lower multiple pregnancy rates than clomifene in PCOS. Lifestyle modification should accompany pharmacological treatment. Metformin alone is insufficient for ovulation induction. Gonadotrophins are second-line, more expensive, and carry higher multiple pregnancy risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Clomifene was previously first-line but has been superseded by letrozole in PCOS-related subfertility per updated NICE NG245 (2023) and international PCOS guidelines — letrozole achieves better live birth rates.

Correct — letrozole is now the recommended first-line ovulation induction agent for PCOS subfertility per NICE NG245 (2023); it has higher live birth rates and fewer multiple pregnancies than clomifene.
Metformin alone does not reliably induce ovulation in most women with PCOS; it may be used as an adjunct to letrozole but not as sole fertility treatment.
Gonadotrophins with IUI are second-line interventions reserved for failure of first-line oral ovulation induction; they carry higher costs and multiple pregnancy risk.
The COCP suppresses ovulation and would prevent conception — it has no role as a bridge to fertility in women actively trying to conceive.
clinicalPearl: Letrozole has replaced clomifene as first-line ovulation induction in anovulatory PCOS infertility per NICE NG245 (2023) — higher live birth rates, lower multiple pregnancy risk.
keyClue: PCOS + BMI 34 + insulin resistance signs + anovulatory subfertility with normal tubes and partner — first-line ovulation induction is letrozole.
commonExamTrap: Choosing clomifene as first-line — this was correct until ~2022 but letrozole is now recommended first per updated NICE and international PCOS guidelines.
missedConcept: Updated PCOS management guidelines (NICE NG245, 2023) now position letrozole as first-line ovulation induction over clomifene, based on superior live birth rates in RCTs.

subtopic: Deep vein thrombosis prophylaxis and VTE
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old woman with BMI 33 kg/m², hypertension, and previous DVT undergoes an elective total abdominal hysterectomy for fibroid uterus. Estimated blood loss is 350 mL. She is recovering on the gynaecology ward. Her Caprini VTE risk score is calculated at 8 (high risk). She had prophylactic LMWH pre-operatively. Which post-operative VTE prophylaxis regimen is MOST appropriate for this patient?
options: Thromboembolic deterrent (TED) stockings alone until discharge
LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC) once daily for 7 days post-operatively
LMWH once daily continued for 28 days post-operatively plus TED stockings
Unfractionated heparin IV infusion for 5 days then switch to warfarin
Aspirin 75 mg orally once daily for 28 days
explanation: For patients undergoing major gynaecological pelvic surgery who are at high VTE risk (Caprini score ≥8, history of DVT, obesity, malignancy or major surgery >30 min), extended LMWH prophylaxis for 28 days post-operatively is recommended (NICE NG89 and RCOG guidelines), combined with anti-embolism stockings. This patient has multiple compounding risk factors. A 7-day course is insufficient for high-risk major pelvic surgery. TED stockings alone and aspirin are inadequate. Unfractionated heparin infusion is not standard post-operative prophylaxis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: TED stockings alone are inadequate for a high-risk patient after major pelvic surgery; pharmacological prophylaxis is mandatory.
Seven days of LMWH is appropriate for moderate-risk patients; this high-risk patient (previous DVT, BMI 33, major pelvic surgery) requires extended 28-day prophylaxis.
Correct — extended LMWH prophylaxis for 28 days post major gynaecological surgery plus TED stockings is recommended for high-risk patients per NICE NG89 and RCOG Green-top guidelines.
UFH infusion transitioning to warfarin is used for therapeutic anticoagulation in established VTE, not for post-operative prophylaxis in routine surgical cases.
Aspirin has no evidence base for VTE prophylaxis after major pelvic surgery and is not recommended in gynaecological post-operative guidelines.
clinicalPearl: High-risk major gynaecological surgery patients (previous VTE, obesity, pelvic surgery >30 min) require extended LMWH prophylaxis for 28 days post-operatively.
keyClue: High VTE risk (Caprini 8, previous DVT, BMI 33) after major gynaecological surgery = 28-day extended LMWH prophylaxis.
commonExamTrap: Selecting 7-day LMWH — this is for moderate-risk patients; high-risk patients after major pelvic surgery require 28-day extended prophylaxis.
missedConcept: Extended post-operative LMWH for 28 days is required for high-risk major pelvic/oncological gynaecological surgery, mirroring the colorectal surgery evidence base.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman is reviewed on day 5 post-laparoscopic hysterectomy for endometriosis. She complains of increasing wound pain, fever (38.2°C), and purulent discharge from the umbilical port site. The wound edges are erythematous, warm, and indurated over a 4 × 3 cm area. WBC 13.6 × 10⁹/L. She is otherwise haemodynamically stable. She received a dose of co-amoxiclav at induction and was discharged on day 2, then re-admitted today. What is the MOST appropriate initial management?
options: Swab wound, start oral flucloxacillin, and reassess in 48 hours
Open and drain the wound, send wound swab for MC&S, start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
Arrange urgent CT abdomen and pelvis to exclude intra-abdominal collection
Apply topical antiseptic dressings and reassess in 24 hours
Re-operate under general anaesthesia for washout
explanation: A superficial surgical site infection with purulent discharge requires opening and draining the wound to remove pus (the surgical principle of 'ubi pus, ibi evacua'), sending wound swab for microbiological culture, and initiating broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (e.g. co-amoxiclav IV or a cephalosporin) given the fever and elevated WBC. Oral antibiotics alone without drainage are inadequate when there is frank pus. CT would be appropriate if an intra-abdominal collection or mesh complication were suspected, but local wound management comes first here.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral flucloxacillin without wound drainage is inadequate when there is purulent discharge — pus in a cavity must be drained surgically before antibiotic penetration is effective.
Correct — wound opening + drainage + wound swab for MC&S + broad-spectrum IV antibiotics is the correct approach for a post-operative wound infection with purulent discharge and systemic features.
CT abdomen and pelvis would be the next step if intra-abdominal collection were suspected, but the presentation is clearly a superficial port-site infection requiring local wound management first.
Topical antiseptics alone are insufficient when pus is present and systemic inflammatory response is evident — this approach delays appropriate treatment.
Return to theatre for full washout is reserved for deep space infection, fascial dehiscence, or necrotising fasciitis — not a superficial port-site SSI.
clinicalPearl: Surgical site infections with purulent discharge require mechanical drainage ('ubi pus, ibi evacua') plus wound swab and systemic antibiotics — topical treatment and oral antibiotics alone are insufficient.
keyClue: Purulent umbilical port-site discharge + erythema + fever + raised WBC = SSI requiring wound opening, drainage, and IV antibiotics.
commonExamTrap: Starting oral antibiotics without draining the wound — pus must be drained for antibiotics to be effective; drainage is the primary intervention.
missedConcept: Post-operative wound infection management follows the principle: drain the collection first, then treat with appropriate antibiotics guided by wound culture.

clinicalStem: A 63-year-old woman, BMI 31 kg/m², presents to her GP with a single episode of per-vaginal bleeding 4 years after her last period. She is not on hormone replacement therapy. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. She has no abdominal pain. Speculum examination is unremarkable. What is the MOST appropriate initial investigation?
options: Cervical smear
Serum CA-125
Pelvic MRI
Transvaginal ultrasound scan to measure endometrial thickness
Diagnostic hysteroscopy under general anaesthesia
explanation: Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. The first-line investigation per NICE (NG12) and RCOG guidelines is transvaginal ultrasound (TVUSS) to measure endometrial thickness. An endometrial thickness ≤4 mm has a negative predictive value >99% for endometrial cancer. If ET is >4 mm or the endometrium is abnormal, hysteroscopy and biopsy are indicated. This patient has additional risk factors for endometrial cancer (obesity, diabetes, hypertension — associated with unopposed oestrogen and insulin resistance).
wrongAnswerExplanations: Cervical smear tests for cervical pathology, not endometrial pathology, and is not the appropriate investigation for postmenopausal bleeding.
CA-125 is a tumour marker for ovarian pathology and is not useful for investigating the endometrium as a cause of postmenopausal bleeding.
Pelvic MRI is used for staging confirmed endometrial carcinoma, not as the initial investigation for postmenopausal bleeding.
Correct — TVUSS measuring endometrial thickness is the first-line investigation for PMB; ET ≤4 mm makes endometrial cancer highly unlikely; ET >4 mm warrants hysteroscopy + biopsy.
Outpatient hysteroscopy is the gold standard for endometrial assessment but is not the first step — TVUSS is performed first to guide further investigation.
clinicalPearl: Postmenopausal bleeding = endometrial cancer until proven otherwise. First investigation: TVUSS. If ET >4 mm, proceed to hysteroscopy and endometrial biopsy.
keyClue: Single episode of vaginal bleeding 4 years after menopause in a woman not on HRT — PMB requiring urgent endometrial assessment.
commonExamTrap: Jumping to hysteroscopy as the answer — TVUSS is the correct first-line step; hysteroscopy follows if ET is thickened or the ultrasound is abnormal.
missedConcept: Endometrial thickness threshold for action in PMB: ≤4 mm = reassure (cancer very unlikely); >4 mm = refer for hysteroscopy and biopsy.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old nulliparous woman presents with 3 months of intermittent postmenopausal bleeding. She is obese (BMI 38), has polycystic ovarian syndrome, and takes tamoxifen for breast cancer. She has never used a combined oral contraceptive or the Mirena IUS. Pelvic TVUSS shows endometrial thickness of 11 mm. Hysteroscopy and biopsy demonstrate Grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma. Which underlying pathophysiological mechanism BEST explains her endometrial cancer risk?
options: Progesterone-driven endometrial hyperplasia from excess progestogen
Chronic unopposed oestrogen stimulation of the endometrium
Mismatch repair gene mutation causing Lynch syndrome
p53 mutation leading to serous endometrial carcinoma
Human papillomavirus (HPV) integration into endometrial cells
explanation: Type 1 endometrioid endometrial cancer (the most common type, Grade 1/2) is driven by unopposed oestrogen stimulation of the endometrium without the protective counter-regulation of progesterone. Risk factors include obesity (peripheral conversion of androgens to oestrogens by aromatase in adipose tissue), nulliparity, PCOS (chronic anovulation = no progesterone production), and tamoxifen (which acts as an oestrogen agonist on the endometrium). These all converge on the same mechanism: excess oestrogen stimulation without progesterone opposition, leading to endometrial hyperplasia and malignant transformation.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Excess progestogen protects against endometrial cancer — the COCP and Mirena IUS are protective by providing progesterone; it is oestrogen without progesterone opposition that drives Type 1 endometrial cancer.
Correct — chronic unopposed oestrogen from obesity (aromatase activity), PCOS (anovulation, no progesterone), and tamoxifen (endometrial agonist) drives Type 1 endometrioid endometrial carcinoma.
Lynch syndrome (MMR gene mutations) is associated with Type 1 endometrial cancer in younger women but is not the primary mechanism here given the constellation of metabolic risk factors described.
p53 mutation drives Type 2 serous endometrial carcinoma (high-grade, oestrogen-independent) — this patient has Grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma, which is Type 1 and oestrogen-dependent.
HPV causes cervical cancer; it plays no role in the pathogenesis of endometrial adenocarcinoma.
clinicalPearl: Type 1 endometrioid endometrial cancer is caused by unopposed oestrogen — obesity, nulliparity, PCOS, tamoxifen, and late menopause all reduce progesterone opposition.
keyClue: Obese + nulliparous + PCOS + tamoxifen + Grade 1 endometrioid = classic Type 1 endometrial cancer driven by unopposed oestrogen.
commonExamTrap: Attributing tamoxifen's endometrial risk to anti-oestrogenic effects — tamoxifen is an oestrogen antagonist at the breast but an agonist at the endometrium.
missedConcept: Tamoxifen acts as a partial oestrogen agonist on the endometrium — women on tamoxifen have a 2–4 fold increased risk of endometrial cancer and must be counselled about PMB.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman, G2P2, has an atonic uterus causing primary PPH (estimated blood loss 2,200 mL) following Caesarean section for placenta praevia. She has received oxytocin 10 units IV, an oxytocin infusion, ergometrine 0.5 mg IM, carboprost 0.25 mg IM (total 3 doses), and misoprostol 800 mcg sublingually. She also received tranexamic acid 1 g IV. The uterus remains poorly contracted. She is haemodynamically unstable: BP 80/50 mmHg, HR 130 bpm. Uterine balloon tamponade has failed. Massive transfusion protocol is activated. What is the MOST appropriate NEXT surgical step?
options: Bilateral uterine artery ligation (O'Leary sutures)
Internal iliac (hypogastric) artery ligation
B-Lynch compression suture
Uterine artery embolisation (UAE)
Peripartum hysterectomy
explanation: This patient has life-threatening intractable PPH despite all medical uterotonics, tranexamic acid, and balloon tamponade failure following Caesarean for placenta praevia. When conservative surgical measures (uterine compression sutures, arterial ligation) and uterine tamponade have failed in a haemodynamically unstable patient, peripartum hysterectomy is the definitive life-saving intervention and must not be delayed. UAE requires a stable, cooperative patient in an interventional radiology suite and is not appropriate for a haemodynamically crashing patient. B-Lynch or arterial ligation are earlier-stage surgical options that should have been attempted before reaching this point — the question describes failure of balloon tamponade, implying all prior steps have been exhausted.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Bilateral uterine artery ligation is a useful early surgical step but should have been performed prior to balloon tamponade; after tamponade failure with haemodynamic collapse, hysterectomy is the priority.

Internal iliac artery ligation is technically difficult, has a high failure rate (40-50%), and in a haemodynamically crashing patient delays definitive treatment — hysterectomy is preferable at this stage.

B-Lynch compression suture is an excellent option for atonic uterus but is an earlier step in the surgical algorithm; after balloon tamponade failure with ongoing collapse, definitive hysterectomy must not be delayed.

UAE requires haemodynamic stability, transfer to interventional radiology, and time — it is contraindicated in active haemodynamic collapse; it is appropriate as a planned post-tamponade procedure or for stable patients.
Correct — peripartum hysterectomy is the definitive life-saving operation when all other medical and conservative surgical measures have failed; delay risks exsanguination.
clinicalPearl: In intractable PPH unresponsive to all uterotonics and tamponade with haemodynamic collapse, peripartum hysterectomy is the life-saving procedure — do not delay.
keyClue: Failed all uterotonics + balloon tamponade failure + haemodynamic collapse (BP 80/50, HR 130) = definitive hysterectomy, not further conservative surgery.
commonExamTrap: Choosing UAE or B-Lynch — UAE requires haemodynamic stability; B-Lynch is an earlier step in the algorithm before tamponade. Hysterectomy is correct when tamponade has failed with collapse.
missedConcept: The PPH surgical escalation ladder: compression sutures (B-Lynch) → arterial ligation → balloon tamponade → UAE (if stable) → hysterectomy (definitive). After tamponade failure with collapse, go directly to hysterectomy.
redFlagReason: Haemorrhagic shock (BP 80/50, HR 130) from intractable PPH — delay in peripartum hysterectomy risks exsanguination and maternal death.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of light per-vaginal bleeding over 6 months. She is 22 years post-menopause and is not on HRT. She complains of vaginal dryness and dyspareunia. On speculum examination, the vaginal walls appear pale, thin, and atrophic with contact bleeding on swab insertion. There are no cervical lesions. TVUSS shows endometrial thickness of 3 mm. Pipelle endometrial biopsy is non-diagnostic (insufficient sample). What is the MOST likely cause of her bleeding?
options: Endometrial carcinoma — biopsy should be repeated
Atrophic vaginitis (genitourinary syndrome of menopause)
Endometrial polyp — hysteroscopy required
Cervical carcinoma — urgent colposcopy required
Hormone-secreting ovarian tumour
explanation: Atrophic vaginitis (now termed genitourinary syndrome of menopause, GSM) is the most common cause of postmenopausal bleeding, responsible for approximately 60–80% of cases. It is characterised by thin, pale, friable vaginal mucosa with contact bleeding, vaginal dryness, and dyspareunia — all present here. The endometrial thickness is 3 mm (below the 4 mm threshold), making endometrial carcinoma highly unlikely. The combination of reassuring ultrasound, atrophic speculum findings, and dyspareunia strongly points to GSM as the diagnosis.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Endometrial carcinoma is highly unlikely with ET 3 mm (negative predictive value >99%); the clinical examination clearly shows atrophic vaginitis as the source of bleeding.

Correct — genitourinary syndrome of menopause (atrophic vaginitis) is the most common cause of PMB, characterised by atrophic pale friable vaginal mucosa, contact bleeding, dryness, and dyspareunia, with a reassuringly thin endometrium.
An endometrial polyp is possible but the atrophic speculum findings directly explain the bleeding; TVUSS at 3 mm makes intrauterine pathology unlikely without a visible polyp.
Cervical carcinoma must be excluded but the speculum shows no cervical lesion and the history of atrophic vaginitis with contact bleeding from vaginal walls is more compelling.
Hormone-secreting ovarian tumours cause hyperoestrogenism leading to endometrial thickening (>4 mm) and hyperplasia — the ET here is 3 mm, making this diagnosis implausible.
clinicalPearl: Atrophic vaginitis (GSM) is the commonest cause of postmenopausal bleeding — pale, thin, friable vaginal walls + contact bleeding + ET <4 mm = treat with topical oestrogen.
keyClue: Atrophic pale vaginal walls + contact bleeding + dyspareunia + ET 3 mm in a woman 22 years post-menopause = genitourinary syndrome of menopause.
commonExamTrap: Assuming any PMB must be cancer — with ET ≤4 mm and clear atrophic changes on examination, GSM is the most likely diagnosis and repeat biopsy is not warranted.
missedConcept: Atrophic vaginitis (GSM) accounts for 60–80% of postmenopausal bleeding; it is treated with topical vaginal oestrogen and is not pre-malignant.
